[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Natron%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Natron%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Natron%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Natron%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

is a naturally occurring mixture of [sodium carbonate](/wiki/Sodium_carbonate "Sodium carbonate") decahydrate ([Na2CO3](/wiki/Sodium_carbonate "Sodium carbonate")·10H2O, a kind of [soda ash](/wiki/Soda_ash "Soda ash")) and around 17% [sodium bicarbonate](/wiki/Sodium_bicarbonate "Sodium bicarbonate") (also called baking soda, NaHCO3) along with small quantities of [sodium chloride](/wiki/Sodium_chloride "Sodium chloride") and [sodium sulfate](/wiki/Sodium_sulfate "Sodium sulfate"). Natron is white to colourless when pure, varying to gray or yellow with impurities. Natron deposits are sometimes found in saline lake beds which arose in arid environments. Throughout history natron has had many practical applications that continue today in the wide range of modern uses of its constituent mineral components.

Carbonate mineral

**Natron** is a naturally occurring mixture of [sodium carbonate](/wiki/Sodium_carbonate "Sodium carbonate") decahydrate ([Na2CO3](/wiki/Sodium_carbonate "Sodium carbonate")·10H2O, a kind of [soda ash](/wiki/Soda_ash "Soda ash")) and around 17% [sodium bicarbonate](/wiki/Sodium_bicarbonate "Sodium bicarbonate") (also called baking soda, NaHCO3) along with small quantities of [sodium chloride](/wiki/Sodium_chloride "Sodium chloride") and [sodium sulfate](/wiki/Sodium_sulfate "Sodium sulfate"). Natron is white to colourless when pure, varying to gray or yellow with impurities. Natron deposits are sometimes found in saline lake beds which arose in arid environments. Throughout history natron has had many practical applications that continue today in the wide range of modern uses of its constituent mineral components.

In modern [mineralogy](/wiki/Mineralogy "Mineralogy") the term *natron* has come to mean only the sodium carbonate decahydrate (hydrated soda ash) that makes up most of the historical salt.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Natron_deposits%2C_Trou_au_Natron%2C_Chad.jpg/250px-Natron_deposits%2C_Trou_au_Natron%2C_Chad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Natron_deposits,_Trou_au_Natron,_Chad.jpg)

Natron deposits, Trou au Natron, Tibesti, Chad

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natron&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") word *natron* is a [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") [cognate](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") derived through the [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") *natrón* from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") *natrium* and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") *nitron* (νίτρον). This derives from the [Ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian "Ancient Egyptian") word [*nṯrj*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/n%E1%B9%AFrj#Egyptian "wikt:nṯrj"). *Natron* refers to [Wadi El Natrun](/wiki/Wadi_El_Natrun "Wadi El Natrun") or Natron Valley in Egypt, from which natron was mined by the ancient Egyptians for use in burial rites. The modern chemical symbol for [sodium](/wiki/Sodium "Sodium"), *Na*, is an abbreviation of that element's [Neo-Latin](/wiki/Neo-Latin "Neo-Latin") name *natrium*, which was derived from *natron*. The name of the chemical element [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") is also a cognate to natron, it derives from the Greek *nitron* and *-gen* (a suffix meaning "producer of", in this case of [nitric acid](/wiki/Nitric_acid "Nitric acid"), which was produced from [niter](/wiki/Niter "Niter") or [nitre] [potassium nitrate]). *Niter* is also an obsolete name for natron because in earlier times, both minerals used to be confused with each other.

## Importance in antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natron&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Importance in antiquity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Egyptian_-_%22Malqata_Kateriskos%22_Vessel_-_Walters_4732_-_Profile.jpg/250px-Egyptian_-_%22Malqata_Kateriskos%22_Vessel_-_Walters_4732_-_Profile.jpg)](/wiki/File:Egyptian_-_%22Malqata_Kateriskos%22_Vessel_-_Walters_4732_-_Profile.jpg)

A [faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience") vase fabricated in part from natron, dating to the [New Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") (c. 1450–1350 BC)

Historical natron was harvested directly as a salt mixture from dry lake beds in [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), and has been used for thousands of years as a cleaning product for both the home and body. Blended with oil, it was an early form of [soap](/wiki/Soap "Soap"). It [softens water](/wiki/Water_softening "Water softening") while removing oil and grease. Undiluted, natron was a cleanser for the teeth and an early [mouthwash](/wiki/Mouthwash "Mouthwash"). The mineral was mixed into early [antiseptics](/wiki/Antiseptics "Antiseptics") for wounds and minor cuts. Natron can be used to dry and preserve fish and meat. It was also an ancient household insecticide, and was used for making leather as well as a bleach for clothing.

The mineral was used during [mummification](/wiki/Mummification "Mummification") ceremonies in ancient Egypt because it absorbs water and behaves as a drying agent. Moreover, when exposed to moisture, the [carbonate](/wiki/Carbonate "Carbonate") in natron increases [pH](/wiki/PH "PH") (raises [alkalinity](/wiki/Alkalinity "Alkalinity")), which creates a hostile environment for bacteria. In some cultures, natron was thought to enhance spiritual safety for both the living and the dead. Natron was added to [castor oil](/wiki/Castor_oil "Castor oil") to make a [smokeless fuel](/wiki/Smokeless_fuel "Smokeless fuel"), which allowed Egyptian artisans to paint elaborate artworks inside ancient tombs without staining them with soot.

The [Pyramid Texts](/wiki/Pyramid_Texts "Pyramid Texts") describe how natron pellets were used as funerary offerings in the rites for the pharaoh "N". The ceremony required two kinds of natron: one sourced from northern (Lower) Egypt and one from southern (Upper) Egypt.

> *Smin*, *smin* opens thy mouth. One pellet of natron.  
> O N., thou shalt taste its taste in front of the *sḥ-ntr-*chapels. One pellet of natron.  
> That which Horus spits out is *smin*. One pellet of natron.  
> That which Set spits out is *smin*. One pellet of natron.  
> That which the two harmonious gods (spit out) is *smin*. One pellet of natron.  
> To say four times: Thou hast purified thyself with natron, together with Horus (and) the Followers of Horus. Five pellets of natron from Nekheb, Upper Egypt.  
> Thou purifiest (thyself); Horus purifies (himself). One pellet of natron. Thou purifiest (thyself); Set purifies (himself). One pellet of natron.  
> Thou purifiest (thyself); Thot purifies (himself). One pellet of natron. Thou purifiest (thyself); the god purifies (himself). One pellet of natron.  
> Thou also purifiest (thyself)—thou who art among them. One pellet of natron.  
> Thy mouth is the mouth of a sucking calf on the day of his birth.  
> Five pellets of natron of the North, Wadi Natrûn (*št-p.t*)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Natron is an ingredient in the making of a distinct color called *[Egyptian blue](/wiki/Egyptian_blue "Egyptian blue")*, and also as the [flux](/wiki/Flux_(metallurgy) "Flux (metallurgy)") in [Egyptian faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience"). It was used along with [sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand") and [lime](/wiki/Lime_(mineral) "Lime (mineral)") in ceramic and [glass making](/wiki/Glass_making "Glass making") by the Romans and others until at least AD 640. The mineral was also employed as a flux to solder precious metals together.

### Decline in use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natron&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Decline in use")]

Most of natron's uses both in the home and by industry were gradually replaced with closely related sodium compounds and minerals. Natron's [detergent](/wiki/Detergent "Detergent") properties are now commercially supplied by [soda ash](/wiki/Soda_ash "Soda ash") (pure sodium carbonate), the mixture's chief compound ingredient, along with other chemicals. Soda ash also replaced natron in the [ancient glass trade](/wiki/Ancient_glass_trade "Ancient glass trade"). Some of its ancient household roles are also now filled by ordinary [baking soda](/wiki/Baking_soda "Baking soda"), which is [sodium bicarbonate](/wiki/Sodium_bicarbonate "Sodium bicarbonate"), natron's other key ingredient.

## Chemistry of hydrated sodium carbonate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natron&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Chemistry of hydrated sodium carbonate")]

*Natron* is also the mineralogical name for the compound sodium carbonate decahydrate (Na2CO3**·**10H2O), which is the main component in historical natron.[[4]](#cite_note-Webmin-4)Sodium carbonate decahydrate has a [specific gravity](/wiki/Specific_gravity "Specific gravity") of 1.42 to 1.47 and a [Mohs hardness](/wiki/Mohs_hardness "Mohs hardness") of 1. It crystallizes in the [monoclinic](/wiki/Monoclinic "Monoclinic")-domatic [crystal system](/wiki/Crystal_system "Crystal system"), typically forming efflorescences and encrustations.

The term *hydrated sodium carbonate* is commonly used to encompass the monohydrate (Na2CO3**·**H2O), the decahydrate and the heptahydrate (Na2CO3**·**7H2O), but is often used in industry to refer to the decahydrate only. Both the hepta- and the decahydrate effloresce (lose water) in dry air and are partially transformed into the monohydrate [thermonatrite](/wiki/Thermonatrite "Thermonatrite") Na2CO3**·**H2O.

### As a source of soda ash

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natron&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: As a source of soda ash")]

Sodium carbonate decahydrate is stable at room temperature but recrystallizes at only 32 °C (90 °F) to sodium carbonate heptahydrate, Na2CO3**·**7H2O, then above 37–38 °C (99–100 °F) to sodium carbonate monohydrate, Na2CO3**·**H2O. This recrystallization from decahydrate to monohydrate releases much crystal water in a mostly clear, colorless salt solution with little solid [thermonatrite](/wiki/Thermonatrite "Thermonatrite"). The mineral natron is often found in association with [thermonatrite](/wiki/Thermonatrite "Thermonatrite"), [nahcolite](/wiki/Nahcolite "Nahcolite"), [trona](/wiki/Trona "Trona"), [halite](/wiki/Halite "Halite"), [mirabilite](/wiki/Mirabilite "Mirabilite"), [gaylussite](/wiki/Gaylussite "Gaylussite"), [gypsum](/wiki/Gypsum "Gypsum"), and [calcite](/wiki/Calcite "Calcite").
Most industrially produced sodium carbonate is soda ash (sodium carbonate anhydrate Na2CO3) which is obtained by calcination (dry heating at temperatures of 150 to 200 °C) of sodium bicarbonate, sodium carbonate monohydrate, or [trona](/wiki/Trona "Trona").

## Geological occurrence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Natron&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geological occurrence")]

Geologically, the mineral natron as well as the historical natron are formed as transpiro-[evaporite](/wiki/Evaporite "Evaporite") minerals, i.e. crystallizing during the drying up of salt lakes rich in sodium carbonate. The sodium carbonate is usually formed by absorption of [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") from the atmosphere by a highly alkaline, sodium-rich lake [brine](/wiki/Brine "Brine"), according to the following reaction scheme:

NaOH(aq) + CO2 → NaHCO3(aq)

NaHCO3(aq) + NaOH(aq) → Na2CO3(aq) + H2O

Pure deposits of sodium carbonate decahydrate are rare, due to the limited temperature stability of this compound and due to the fact that the absorption of [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") usually produces mixtures of [bicarbonate](/wiki/Bicarbonate "Bicarbonate") and [carbonate](/wiki/Carbonate "Carbonate") in solution. From such mixtures, the mineral natron (and also the historical one) will be formed only if the [brine](/wiki/Brine "Brine") temperature during evaporation is maximally about 20 °C (68 °F) – or the [alkalinity](/wiki/Alkalinity "Alkalinity") of the lake is so high, that little [bicarbonate](/wiki/Bicarbonate "Bicarbonate") is present in solution (see reaction scheme above) – in which case the maximum temperature is increased to about 30 °C (86 °F). In most cases the mineral natron will form together with some amount of [nahcolite](/wiki/Nahcolite "Nahcolite") ([sodium bicarbonate](/wiki/Sodium_bicarbonate "Sodium bicarbonate")), resulting in salt mixtures like the historical natron.
Otherwise, the minerals [trona](/wiki/Trona "Trona")[[6]](#cite_note-6) or [thermonatrite](/wiki/Thermonatrite "Thermonatrite") and [nahcolite](/wiki/Nahcolite "Nahcolite") are commonly formed. As the evaporation of a salt lake will occur over geological time spans, during which also part or all of the salt beds might redissolve and recrystallize, deposits of sodium carbonate can be composed of layers of all these minerals.

The following list may include geographical sources of either natron or other hydrated sodium carbonate minerals:

* Africa
  + [Chad](/wiki/Chad "Chad")
    - shores of [Lake Chad](/wiki/Lake_Chad "Lake Chad")
    - [Trou au Natron](/wiki/Trou_au_Natron "Trou au Natron")
    - Era Kohor crater on [Emi Koussi](/wiki/Emi_Koussi "Emi Koussi")
  + [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt")
    - [Wadi El Natrun](/wiki/Wadi_El_Natrun "Wadi El Natrun") (Natron Valley)
  + [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia")
    - [Showa Province](/wiki/Shewa "Shewa")
  + [Niger](/wiki/Niger "Niger")
    - [Bilma](/wiki/Bilma "Bilma")
  + [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania") and [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya")
    - [Lake Natron](/wiki/Lake_Natron "Lake Natron")
* Europe
  + [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary")
    - [Bács-Kiskun County](/wiki/B%C3%A1cs-Kiskun "Bács-Kiskun"), (Great Hungarian Plain)
    - [Szabolcs-Szatmár-Bereg County](/wiki/Szabolcs-Szatm%C3%A1r-Bereg "Szabolcs-Szatmár-Bereg") (Great Hungarian Plain)
  + [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy")
    - [Campania](/wiki/Campania "Campania")
    - [Province of Naples](/wiki/Province_of_Naples "Province of Naples")
    - [Somma-Vesuvius](/wiki/Somma-Vesuvius "Somma-Vesuvius") Complex
  + [Kola Peninsula](/wiki/Kola_Peninsula "Kola Peninsula"), northern [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia")
    - [Khibiny Massif](/wiki/Khibiny_Massif "Khibiny Massif")
    - [Lovozero Massif](/wiki/Lovozero_Massif "Lovozero Massif")
      * [Alluaiv](/w/index.php?title=Alluaiv&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alluaiv (page does not exist)") Mountain
      * [Kedykverpakhk](/w/index.php?title=Kedykverpakhk&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kedykverpakhk (page does not exist)") Mountain
    - [Umbozero](/wiki/Umbozero "Umbozero") Mine
  + [England](/wiki/England "England"), UK
    - [St Just District](/wiki/St_Just_in_Penwith "St Just in Penwith")
    - [Botallack](/wiki/Botallack "Botallack") – [Pendeen](/wiki/Pendeen "Pendeen") Area
* North America
  + [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada")
    - [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec")
      * [Rouville County](/wiki/Rouville_Regional_County_Municipality "Rouville Regional County Municipality")
      * [Mont-Saint-Hilaire](/wiki/Mont-Saint-Hilaire,_Quebec "Mont-Saint-Hilaire, Quebec")
    - Interior [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia")
  + [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")
    - [California](/wiki/California "California")
      * [Inyo County](/wiki/Inyo_County,_California "Inyo County, California")
    - [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada")
      * [Churchill County](/wiki/Churchill_County,_Nevada "Churchill County, Nevada") ([Soda Lake District](/wiki/Soda_Lakes "Soda Lakes"))
      * [Humboldt County](/wiki/Humboldt_County,_Nevada "Humboldt County, Nevada")
      * [Mineral County](/wiki/Mineral_County,_Nevada "Mineral County, Nevada")
    - [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon")
      * [Lake County](/wiki/Lake_County,_Oregon "Lake County, Oregon")
    - [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania")
      * [Karns](/wiki/Karns,_Pennsylvania "Karns, Pennsylvania")
      * [Natrona](/wiki/Natrona,_Pennsylvania "Natrona, Pennsylvania")
    - [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)")
      * [Okanogan County](/wiki/Okanogan_County,_Washington "Okanogan County, Washington")
    - [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming")
      * [Natrona County](/wiki/Natrona_County,_Wyoming "Natrona County, Wyoming")
